abstract: AI code generators, such as OpenAI Codex, offer promising support for novice programmers by translating natural language into functional code. However, concerns remain that an over-reliance on these tools could hinder long-term learning and retention. To investigate these implications within introductory programming, we conducted a controlled experiment involving 69 students (ages 10–17). Participants completed 45 Python code-authoring tasks—with half the group using Codex—followed by subsequent code-modification challenges. Our findings indicate that Codex significantly boosted authoring performance, resulting in a 1.15x higher completion rate and scores 1.8x higher than the control group, without undermining performance on manual modification tasks. Furthermore, learners who used Codex during training performed slightly better on evaluation post-tests held one week later, though this trend was not statistically significant. Notably, students with higher initial Scratch proficiency showed significantly better retention if they had prior access to Codex, suggesting that AI assistance may particularly benefit those with existing foundational logic.
